recap: For d.p., count digits after the decimal point; for s.f., count from the first non-zero digit — in both cases, look at the next digit and round up if it is 5 or more.

steps: 2 d.p. means we keep two digits after the decimal point: 8.47|62.
The digit after the 2nd d.p. is 6 — round up. The 7 becomes 8.
(a) 1 s.f.: first digit is 3. Next digit is 8 — round up. 3 becomes 4, rest become zeros: 40,000.
(b) 3 s.f.: first three digits are 3, 8, 5. Next digit is 4 — round down. Answer: 38,500.

questions.question: A calculator gives the answer 0.079461. Round this to (a) 3 d.p.  (b) 2 s.f.

steps: (a) 3 d.p.: 0.079|461. Third d.p. digit is 9. Next digit is 4 — round down. Answer: 0.079.
(b) 2 s.f.: first s.f. is 7 (the first non-zero digit), second is 9. Next digit is 4 — round down. Answer: 0.079.

questions.question: A length is measured as 3.996 m. Round this to 2 decimal places. Explain why this answer might cause confusion.

steps: 3.99|6: next digit is 6 — round up. 3.99 rounds up to 4.00.
This could cause confusion because it looks like the measurement is exactly 4 m, when in fact it was rounded — the trailing zeros show the precision.
solution.final_answer: 4.00 m. The zeros indicate the value is given to 2 d.p., not that it is exactly 4 m.

questions.question: A number rounded to 3 significant figures is 4,720. Write down the smallest and largest values the number could have been before rounding.

steps: 4,720 to 3 s.f. means the 4th significant figure (the zero in the units column) was the digit that was looked at.
For rounding to give 4,720, the number before rounding was in the range from 4,715 up to (but not including) 4,725.
Smallest possible value: 4,715. Largest possible value: less than 4,725 (the upper bound is 4,725 but it would round up to 4,730).
solution.final_answer: Smallest: 4,715. Largest value that rounds to 4,720: any value up to but not including 4,725 (upper bound = 4,725).

summary: Decimal places are counted from the decimal point. Significant figures are counted from the first non-zero digit.
Look one digit beyond the position you are rounding to: 5 or more rounds up, 4 or less rounds down.
Trailing zeros after a decimal point are significant and must be kept when a specific number of d.p. is required.
Leading zeros (before the first non-zero digit) are never significant.
